lesson.menu_title: More, Less, the Best -- Comparatives and Superlatives
lesson.menu_desc: Compare universities, hotels and conferences -- bigger, more formal, the best -- 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 8 -- More, Less, the Best -- Comparatives and Superlatives
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Clara | Lesson 8 -- Comparatives & Superlatives

listenings.text: Hello, Maria Clara, this is Daniel from the council office. I have two hotel options for your next trip. The first hotel is more comfortable and more modern, but it is more expensive and more distant from the university. The second hotel is smaller and simpler, but it is cheaper and much closer to the campus. In my opinion, the second one is the better choice for a short trip. Please tell me which one you prefer. Thank you, and have a good day.

Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the annual conference. This year, our event is bigger than ever. We have more delegations than last year, and more speakers from distant countries. The program is also more formal, with three round tables instead of two. The most important session is on Friday morning, so please do not miss it. This is our most significant conference in ten years. Thank you all, and enjoy the week.

extra_audio.text: First, the university in Seoul was larger than ours. Then, the conference was more formal than the previous one. The best moment was the final round table. And my current English was better than last year.

paras: Last month, Maria Clara visited two universities for the council. The university in Seoul is much larger than her university in Brazil. It is also more modern and more efficient: the rooms are bigger, and the technology is newer. Her own university is older and more traditional. In her opinion, it is warmer and more comfortable. "The Seoul campus is the most impressive place on my trip," she wrote in her report.

This year's conference was more formal than the previous one. It was also more significant, because more delegations came from distant countries. The accommodation was better than last time. The hotel was more comfortable, but it was also more expensive and more distant from the campus. Maria Clara would rather stay in a smaller, closer hotel. In her opinion, a local hotel is more practical than a large one far away.

The best moment was the final round table. It was the most important meeting of the week, and Maria Clara spoke in English, with no interpreter. Her current English is better than her English last year, and every month it is stronger. "The more I practice, the better I speak," she thought. That night, she called her granddaughter Clarinha. "Grandma spoke English today -- better than yesterday!" They both smiled.
reading.source: A delegation report written for this course

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Maria Clara compares two universities and two conferences, and her English is getting better.
Maria Clara did not like her trip and wants to stop traveling for the council.
Maria Clara only writes about the food and the weather in Seoul.

items: The university in Seoul is larger than Maria Clara's university in Brazil.
The first paragraph says the university in Seoul is much larger than her university in Brazil.
This year's conference was less formal than the previous one.
The second paragraph says this year's conference was MORE formal than the previous one, not less.
The hotel was more comfortable, but also more expensive and more distant from the campus.
The second paragraph says the hotel was more comfortable, but more expensive and more distant.
At the final round table, Maria Clara spoke with an interpreter.
The third paragraph says she spoke in English, with no interpreter.

items.situation: A colleague asks: "How was the conference this year?"
tips: It was more formal and more significant than last year.

items.situation: Someone asks: "Which university is bigger -- Seoul or yours?"
tips: The university in Seoul is larger than mine.

items.situation: You compare two hotels for the next trip.
tips: This hotel is more comfortable, but the other one is cheaper.
Use more + adjective for long words.

items.situation: A colleague asks: "What was the best part of the week?"
tips: The final round table was the best moment.

items.situation: Someone asks about your English now.
tips: My current English is better than last year.

items.situation: You choose between a large hotel far away and a small local one.
tips: I would rather stay in a local hotel.
I would rather + base verb = your preference.
